title: Baking Soda Pre-Treatment for Darker Engraving on Wood
body:
Applying a baking soda solution to wood before engraving raises the surface pH, making it more reactive to heat. The result is a darker, richer burn — closer to deep brown or near-black — without excessive charring, and with cleaner lines on softwoods.

Best suited for: pine, basswood, maple, birch plywood. Avoid oily or dense hardwoods (walnut, teak) — the oil resists the solution and results are unpredictable.

Mix 1 tablespoon of baking soda into 1 cup of warm water and stir until dissolved. Spray or brush a light, even coat over the area to be engraved. The wood should be damp but not soaked. You can engrave while still slightly damp, or let it dry fully — both work. Make the solution fresh each session; it loses effectiveness if stored.

Use your normal engraving settings as a starting point. The treatment amplifies the burn, so try a slightly higher speed or lower power on your first test piece to avoid over-burning. Lower DPI with slower speed tends to produce the darkest, most dramatic results.

After engraving, lightly sand around the engraved area to remove any dried residue from the untreated surface. Apply a finish or sealant to lock in contrast and protect the piece. If the solution was applied unevenly, some areas may show patchy discolouration — consistent application is the main technique variable to control.
